relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Israel gathers as one body around the evidence of atrocity, but the woman whose body summoned them cannot speak.
Leaders and an immense armed assembly stand before Divine.
Benjamin hears of the gathering while Israel asks how the evil happened.
The Levite identifies himself through the murdered woman and begins a testimony shaped around his own survival.
He names Gibeah's violence but leaves his own betrayal outside the account.
He admits cutting and distributing her body, turning private horror into national mobilization.
The assembly is asked to speak and counsel.
Unity hardens quickly into a refusal to return home.
The people decide by lot to move against Gibeah.
An army organizes provisions to answer outrage with force.
All Israel joins tightly as one man, while the missing woman remains unseen within that unity.
Messengers confront Benjamin with the evil sheltered inside the tribe.
A demand for the perpetrators is refused, and kinship becomes protection for violence.
Benjamin gathers for war rather than release the accused men.
Cities become troop counts, and accountability becomes tribal confrontation.
Seven hundred left-handed slingers embody precision; bodily difference carries no shame.
Israel's overwhelming numbers deepen the scale of what is about to unfold.
The assembly asks who will begin, and Judah is named first.
Israel camps against Gibeah at morning.
Battle lines replace the possibility of surrender and accountable judgment.
Benjamin kills twenty-two thousand Israelites in the first collision.
Loss does not create restraint; the survivors strengthen themselves for another battle.
Israel weeps and names Benjamin as brother, yet receives another command to approach.
The second day begins.
Another eighteen thousand fall, and grief multiplies without repairing the original harm.
The whole people weep, fast, and offer before YHWH.
They inquire again beside the ark of covenant.
Phinehas asks whether battle should continue, and tomorrow's victory is promised.
Israel places ambushes around Gibeah.
A third battle repeats the visible pattern while concealing a new strategy.
Benjamin is drawn away and sees thirty more Israelites fall along the roads.
Confidence in repetition blinds Benjamin to the trap.
Israel rises at Baal-tamar as the hidden force breaks from cover.
Ten thousand chosen fighters strike, and disaster arrives before Benjamin recognizes it.
The narrative attributes defeat to YHWH while human weapons destroy thousands.
Israel's retreat is revealed as bait for the ambush.
The hidden force rushes Gibeah and kills through the city.
Smoke becomes the agreed signal that the city is burning.
Benjamin mistakes another thirty deaths for proof that the first victories are returning.
The city rises as smoke behind them.
Israel turns, and Benjamin sees catastrophe closing from both directions.
Flight toward wilderness cannot escape the converging violence.
The army surrounds, pursues, and crushes its brother tribe.
Eighteen thousand more lives are counted as fallen strength.
The pursuit continues along roads and toward Rimmon, taking thousands who flee.
The day's total becomes a near-erasure of Benjamin's fighting population.
Six hundred survivors reach the rock and remain there four months.
Israel turns from battle to settlements, killing inhabitants and animals and burning every city it finds.

contemplative_note: Judges 20 shows moral outrage becoming collective annihilation. A survivor's absent voice is replaced by an armed man's partial testimony; protection of perpetrators becomes tribal war; prayer and oracle are carried into ambush; and a demand for accountability ends in burned towns, dead animals, fugitives hunted, and a people almost erased. The contemplative test is whether justice still sees the vulnerable once anger becomes unanimous. If it does not, unity itself can become an idol.
